name: Arm Circles
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and extend both arms straight out to your sides at shoulder height, forming a T-shape with your body.
Begin making small, controlled circles with both arms simultaneously, moving forward. Keep your core lightly engaged and your shoulders relaxed — do not shrug them up toward your ears.
Gradually increase the size of the circles over 8 reps until you are making large, sweeping circles that challenge your shoulder range of motion.
After completing 15 forward circles, reverse direction and perform 15 backward circles, again starting small and gradually getting larger.
Keep your breathing steady throughout. If you feel tension in your neck, drop your chin slightly and relax your jaw.
cues: Keep your arms as straight as possible throughout — avoid bending the elbows
Stand tall: chest lifted, core lightly braced, feet grounded
Start small and build bigger — do not start with large swings
modification: If shoulder discomfort occurs, reduce the circle size or bend your elbows slightly to reduce lever length.

name: Hip Circles
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and place both hands on your hips. Slightly bend your knees to soften the joints.
Begin rotating your hips in a large, slow circle — push them forward, then to the right, then back, then to the left, creating a smooth circular motion.
Think of drawing a big oval on the floor with your hips. Keep your upper body as still as possible — the movement comes purely from the hips and pelvis.
Complete 10 full circles in one direction, then reverse and perform 10 circles in the opposite direction.
Breathe normally throughout and move at a pace that lets you feel the full range of motion in each direction.
cues: Let your hips drive the movement — your shoulders should barely move
Move slowly and deliberately to warm up the hip joint fully
Relax your belly — gripping your abs tightly will restrict the range of motion
modification: Hold onto a wall or chair with one hand for balance if needed.

name: Leg Swings
instructions: Stand sideways next to a wall or sturdy surface. Place your right hand lightly on the wall for balance. Shift your weight onto your right foot.
Swing your left leg forward and backward in a controlled pendulum motion. Start with a small range of motion and gradually increase the swing height over the first few reps.
Your leg should swing naturally — do not force it higher than your comfortable range. Your standing leg stays slightly soft, not locked.
After 12 front-to-back swings, turn to face the wall and swing your leg side to side (across your body and out to the side) for 12 more reps.
Switch legs and repeat both directions on the right leg.
cues: Let momentum do the work — do not muscle the leg higher than it naturally goes
Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning to compensate for the swing
This is a mobility drill, not a stretch — do not hold at the top
modification: Hold the wall with both hands and reduce the swing height if balance is challenging.

name: Cat-Cow Stretch
instructions: Start on all fours on your mat — hands directly under your shoulders and knees directly under your hips. Your spine is in a neutral, flat position (tabletop).
For Cow: Inhale slowly. As you breathe in, let your belly drop toward the floor, lift your chest and chin gently upward, and allow your tailbone to rise slightly. Your back arches gently downward.
For Cat: Exhale slowly. As you breathe out, round your spine up toward the ceiling like an angry cat. Tuck your chin toward your chest and tuck your tailbone under. Your back arches upward.
Flow between these two positions smoothly and continuously, letting your breath guide the movement — inhale into Cow, exhale into Cat.
Move through 10 full breath cycles. This is about spinal mobility, not stretching hard — find a comfortable range and breathe into it.
cues: Let your breath lead the movement — each position should be timed with an inhale or exhale
Move through your entire spine from tailbone to neck, not just one spot
Keep your arms straight and weight evenly distributed between hands and knees
modification: If wrist discomfort occurs, make fists or use forearms on the mat instead of flat hands.

name: Thoracic Rotation (Thread the Needle)
instructions: Start on all fours on your mat — hands under shoulders, knees under hips, spine neutral.
Place your right hand behind your head, elbow pointing out to the side. Keep your left hand firmly planted on the mat for support.
Inhale to prepare. As you exhale, rotate your right elbow downward, threading it under your left arm across your body. Let your right shoulder drop toward the mat as far as comfortable.
Inhale and reverse the movement, rotating your right elbow up toward the ceiling, opening your chest and following it with your eyes. This is the full rotation — feel it in your mid and upper back.
Complete all reps on the right side, then switch: left hand behind head, right hand planted, repeat the threading motion on the left side.
cues: The rotation comes from your upper back (thoracic spine), not your lower back or hips
Keep your hips level and square — do not let them rotate or shift
Follow your elbow with your eyes to encourage the full rotational range
modification: Reduce the rotation range if you feel tightness — even a small range of motion is beneficial for warm-up purposes.

name: Push-Up
instructions: Start in a high plank position — hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, arms straight, core tight, body in a straight line from head to heels. Do not let your hips sag or pike up.
Take a breath in. Begin lowering your body toward the floor by bending your elbows at roughly a 45-degree angle away from your torso (not flared wide, not pinned to your sides).
Lower until your chest is approximately 1–2 inches from the floor or as low as you can control. Your whole body should move as one unit — do not lead with your hips.
Press through your palms to push back up to the starting position, fully extending your arms. Exhale as you press up.
Reset your form at the top before the next rep — check that your hips are neither sagging nor raised and your core is still engaged.
cues: Keep your body in a rigid plank — imagine a broomstick resting along your spine, hips, and head
Elbows at roughly 45 degrees — not straight out like a T or pressed tight to your ribs
Full range of motion: lower with control, press with power
modification: Drop to your knees for a kneeling push-up, keeping the same straight line from knees to head. This reduces the load significantly while maintaining the movement pattern.

name: Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back on the mat with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Your feet should be close enough that you can just graze your heels with your fingertips when arms are at your sides.
Press your lower back gently into the mat to engage your core. Take a breath in to prepare.
As you exhale, press through both heels and squeeze your glutes to lift your hips off the floor. Drive your hips upward until your body forms a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
Squeeze your glutes firmly at the top — hold for 1–2 seconds. Do not overextend by pushing your lower back into an arch; the movement should end when your hips are in line with your torso.
Inhale and slowly lower your hips back to the floor with control. Lightly touch the floor and immediately begin the next rep without relaxing fully.
cues: Drive through your heels, not your toes — if you wiggle your toes, you should still be able to lift
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top — the hip height is secondary to the glute contraction
Do not arch your lower back at the top: the goal is a straight line, not a big curve
modification: Place a folded blanket or pillow under your hips and use a smaller range of motion if you feel any discomfort.

name: Bodyweight Squat
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart (or slightly wider), toes turned out 15–30 degrees. Arms can be extended in front of you for balance or crossed at your chest.
Take a breath in. Begin the squat by pushing your hips back first (as if reaching for a chair behind you), then bend your knees to lower down.

Descend until your thighs are as close to parallel to the floor as comfortable — or lower if mobility allows. Keep your chest tall, spine neutral, and knees tracking over your second toe (not caving inward).
At the bottom, pause briefly. Then press through your full foot — heel, mid-foot, and ball — to drive back up to standing.
Exhale on the way up. Fully extend your hips and knees at the top before beginning the next rep.
cues: Hips back first, then knees bend — this keeps your weight back over your heels
Chest stays tall throughout — if you are rounding forward, squat to a shallower depth
Knees track over your toes — actively push them out if they tend to collapse inward
modification: Squat to a chair or box behind you — touch the surface lightly and stand back up. This limits depth and gives you a target.

name: Forward Lunge
instructions: Stand tall with feet together and hands on your hips or hanging at your sides.
Take a large step forward with your right foot — roughly 2–3 feet, depending on your height. Your right foot should land flat on the floor.

Lower your back (left) knee toward the floor by bending both knees simultaneously. Your right thigh should reach parallel to the floor and your right knee should be directly over your right ankle, not shooting forward past your toes.
Push through your right heel to drive yourself back up to the starting standing position. Bring your right foot back next to your left.
Repeat on the left side. Alternate legs for each rep, counting each side as one rep.
cues: Stay tall through your torso — avoid leaning forward over your front thigh
Front knee stays behind or directly above your front ankle — do not let it drift forward
Control the descent — do not let your back knee crash into the floor
modification: Perform a shorter step and reduce the depth of the lunge. Hold onto a wall or chair for balance support.

name: Wall Sit
instructions: Find a flat, clear wall. Stand with your back flat against the wall, feet about 2 feet in front of you and shoulder-width apart.

Slide your back down the wall by bending your knees until your thighs are parallel to the floor — as if sitting in an invisible chair. Your knees should be directly above your ankles, not pushed forward past your feet.
Keep your entire back flat against the wall throughout — do not let your lower back peel away from the wall or arch. Your head, shoulders, and entire back should maintain contact.
Hold this position for the full 30 seconds. Breathe steadily — do not hold your breath. Keep your core lightly engaged.
To come out of the position, simply slide back up the wall to standing. Do not push off your thighs with your hands.
cues: Keep your back fully flat on the wall — this is what makes it back-safe
90-degree angle at the knees is ideal, but go shallower (less bend) if needed
Breathe steadily and keep your hands resting on your thighs, not pushing
modification: Slide down to only a 45-degree knee bend instead of 90 degrees — this significantly reduces the quad load.

name: Step-Back Lunge
instructions: Stand tall with feet together and hands on your hips. This is the safer alternative to a forward lunge for the lower back because the torso stays more upright.
Take a large step backward with your right foot. Land on the ball of your right foot, keeping your right heel off the floor throughout the movement.
Bend both knees to lower yourself toward the floor. Your left (front) thigh should approach parallel to the floor and your left knee should stay directly above your left ankle.
Keep your torso completely upright — the step-back lunge naturally encourages this. Your chest should face forward, not tilt down.
Drive through your left heel to step your right foot back to the starting position. Repeat on the other side. Alternate for each rep.
cues: Step back far enough that your front knee stays over your ankle — not pushed forward
Keep your torso completely vertical throughout — this is the key advantage of step-back lunges
Control the lowering phase — 2 seconds down, 1 second up
modification: Hold onto a wall or countertop for balance support, or reduce the depth of the lunge.

name: Bird Dog
instructions: Start on all fours on your mat — hands directly under your shoulders, knees directly under your hips, spine in a neutral flat position (not arched or rounded). This is your tabletop position.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, simultaneously extend your right arm straight forward (thumb pointing up) and your left leg straight back behind you. Keep both limbs parallel to the floor.
Hold this extended position for 2 seconds. Your hips must stay perfectly level — do not let the side with the raised leg tilt or rotate. Think of balancing a cup of water on your lower back.
Inhale and return both limbs slowly to the starting position. Your hand and knee should hover just above the floor before the next rep.
Repeat on the opposite side (left arm + right leg). Each pair of extensions counts as one rep.
cues: Keep your hips perfectly square and level — this is the hardest and most important part
Extend long through your fingertips and heel, not just lifting the limbs
Brace your core before you move — pretend you're about to be poked in the belly
modification: Instead of extending the arm and leg simultaneously, extend only the leg while keeping both hands on the floor. Master leg-only first, then add the arm.

name: Dead Bug
instructions: Lie flat on your back on the mat with arms pointing straight up toward the ceiling and knees bent at 90 degrees directly above your hips (tabletop position).
Take a deep breath in. As you exhale, slowly lower your right arm overhead toward the floor while simultaneously straightening and lowering your left leg toward the floor.
Lower both limbs until they are just above the floor — do not let your lower back arch away from the mat. If it arches, don't go as low.
Inhale and return both limbs to the starting position in a controlled manner.
Repeat on the opposite side (left arm + right leg) — that is one full rep.
cues: Press your lower back firmly into the mat throughout the entire movement
Move slowly — this is not a speed exercise, it is a control exercise
Breathe out as you lower, breathe in as you return
modification: Instead of fully extending your leg, only lower it halfway down and keep a slight bend in the knee.

name: Bicep Curl
instructions: Stand tall with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, palms facing forward. Keep your shoulders back and down — do not let them round forward.
Keeping your elbows pinned close to your sides and your upper arms completely still, exhale and curl both dumbbells upward by bending at the elbow.
Curl until the dumbbells are at shoulder height and you feel a strong contraction in your biceps. Your elbows should not drift forward past your torso.
Pause at the top for one second, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position over 2–3 seconds. Control the descent — do not let gravity drop them.
Repeat for all reps. If you cannot keep your upper arms still, the weight is too heavy — switch to the 10lb dumbbells.
cues: Upper arms stay pinned to your sides — only your forearms should move
Squeeze the bicep hard at the top before lowering
Lower slowly — the downward phase builds as much strength as the upward phase
modification: Curl one arm at a time (alternating curls) to reduce total load and focus on each arm individually.

name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
instructions: Stand or sit tall. Hold a 15lb dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward. Your elbows should be at roughly 90 degrees, just below or at ear level.

Take a breath in. As you exhale, press both dumbbells straight up overhead until your arms are fully extended but not locked. The dumbbells should travel in a slight inward arc as they meet near the top.
At the top, your arms should be directly overhead (not leaning forward). Pause briefly.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position at shoulder height. Control the weight — do not let it fall.
Keep your core braced throughout and avoid arching your lower back by tucking your pelvis slightly. If you feel lower back strain, sit on a bench or switch to 10lb dumbbells.
cues: Keep your core braced — do not arch your lower back to get the weight up
Press the weights straight up — avoid pressing forward in front of your head
Control the lowering phase — 2 seconds down keeps the muscles working
modification: Use 10lb dumbbells and perform seated with your back supported against a wall for extra spinal support.

name: Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand tall with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a 10lb dumbbell in each hand at your sides, palms facing inward. Keep a very slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, raise both arms out to your sides simultaneously, leading with your elbows (not your hands). Think of pouring water out of a jug — your pinky side leads slightly.
Raise until your arms are at shoulder height — parallel to the floor. Do not raise above shoulder level, as this adds unnecessary shoulder impingement risk.
Pause for one second at the top, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to your sides over 2 seconds.
Do not use momentum or shrug your shoulders to help the weight up. If you cannot maintain control, use lighter weight or perform one arm at a time.
cues: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — this targets the lateral deltoid more effectively
Stop at shoulder height — higher is not better and can stress the shoulder joint
No swinging — if you are swinging your body to get the weight up, go lighter
modification: Perform one arm at a time while bracing the other hand against your thigh for control. Reduce to lighter resistance if 10lb is too heavy.

name: Bent-Over Dumbbell Row
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Hinge at your hips (push them back, not down) to bring your torso to roughly 45 degrees — keep your spine completely neutral, not rounded. Hold a 15lb dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging straight down.
Take a breath in and brace your core firmly. As you exhale, drive both elbows back and up, rowing the dumbbells toward your lower ribcage.
At the top of the row, your elbows should be close to your torso and pulled behind your back. Squeeze your shoulder blades together firmly.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells with control back to the starting hanging position. Do not let them drop.
Maintain your hip-hinge position for all reps — do not stand up between reps. If you feel your lower back, re-brace your core and ensure your hips are truly hinged, not squatting.
cues: Spine must stay neutral — round back under load is the risk to avoid; brace hard before each rep
Drive elbows back, not up — think of putting your elbows in your back pockets
Keep the dumbbells close to your body, not swinging wide away from your torso
modification: Perform a single-arm row with your free hand resting on a table or chair for spinal support. This allows you to keep a more neutral spine more easily.

name: Goblet Squat
instructions: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, toes turned out 30–45 degrees. Hold a 10lb dumbbell vertically at your chest with both hands cupped around one end, elbows pointing down.
Take a breath in and brace your core. Begin the squat by pushing your hips back and out, then bend your knees to lower yourself between your legs.
Descend as deep as comfortable — aiming for thighs parallel to the floor or lower. Your elbows should track inside your knees as you descend; use them to gently push your knees outward.
Keep your chest tall and the dumbbell close to your body throughout the entire movement. Your heels must stay flat on the floor.
Drive through your heels to press back up to standing, exhaling on the way up. Squeeze your glutes at the top.
cues: Use your elbows to push your knees out — this opens the hips and protects the knees
Keep the dumbbell close to your chest — letting it drift forward pulls you forward
Heels stay down — if they rise, you need to work on ankle mobility or widen your stance
modification: Reduce the squat depth and hold the dumbbell at your chest with two hands close to your body, squatting to a chair for reference.

name: Tricep Kickback
instructions: Stand next to a sturdy surface (chair or table) for support. Hinge at the hips to bring your torso to roughly 45 degrees, keeping your spine neutral and core braced. Place your free hand on the support surface.
Hold a 10lb dumbbell in your working hand. Bring your upper arm up until it is parallel to the floor and pinned close to your side. Your elbow is bent at 90 degrees — this is the starting position.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, extend your forearm backward by straightening your elbow until your entire arm is parallel to the floor and straight.
Squeeze your tricep hard at the fully extended position for one second. Do not let your upper arm drop — it stays parallel to the floor throughout.
Inhale and slowly return your forearm to the 90-degree bent starting position. Complete all reps on one side, then switch arms.
cues: Upper arm stays parallel to the floor and does not swing — only your forearm moves
Squeeze the tricep hard at full extension before returning
Keep your core braced to protect your spine in the hinge position
modification: Use a lighter resistance band or perform with no weight first to learn the movement pattern.

name: Floor Chest Press
instructions: Lie on your back on the mat. Hold a 15lb dumbbell in each hand at chest level, elbows bent at 90 degrees and resting on the floor. Feet are flat on the floor, knees bent. The floor limits how far your elbows can drop, which actually protects your shoulders.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, press both dumbbells straight up toward the ceiling, extending your arms fully. The dumbbells should travel on a slight inward arc and nearly touch at the top.
Hold briefly at the top — arms extended, dumbbells over your chest.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back down until your elbows touch the floor lightly. Control this descent — do not let the weights drop with gravity.
Touch the floor briefly and immediately begin the next press without relaxing your chest. That contact point is just a depth reference.
cues: Press up and slightly inward — imagine pushing the dumbbells together at the top
Lower until elbows touch the floor — this is your natural stopping point on the floor
Keep your shoulder blades pressed into the mat throughout
modification: Use 10lb dumbbells and a narrower grip width if shoulder discomfort occurs.

name: Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift (Light)
instructions: Stand tall holding a 10lb dumbbell in your right hand at your side. Shift your weight onto your left foot. Soften your left knee slightly — do not lock it.

Take a breath in and brace your core. Hinge forward at the hips — push your hips straight back as you lower the dumbbell down your left shin. Your right leg lifts behind you as a counterbalance, staying in line with your torso.
Lower until you feel a stretch in your left hamstring or your torso is parallel to the floor — whichever comes first. Keep your spine completely neutral throughout; do not round your back.
Exhale and drive through your left heel to return to standing, squeezing your left glute at the top. Bring your right foot back to the floor for balance.
Complete all reps on the left side, then switch — hold the dumbbell in your left hand and balance on your right foot.
cues: Hinge at the hip, not the waist — your spine stays long and neutral as your hips push back
Think of your torso and raised leg as a seesaw — they should move in sync
This is a balance exercise too — slow down if you are wobbling
modification: Hold onto a wall lightly with your free hand for balance, or keep both feet on the ground and hinge to a moderate depth (not a full single-leg RDL).

name: Dumbbell Reverse Fly
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Hinge at your hips to bring your torso to roughly 45 degrees — spine neutral, core braced. Hold a 10lb dumbbell in each hand hanging straight down with palms facing each other.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, raise both arms out to your sides simultaneously, leading with your elbows. Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout.
Raise until your arms are parallel to the floor or as high as you can comfortably go. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position with full control.
Maintain your hinge position throughout all reps. If you feel your lower back fatiguing, reduce your torso angle (stand more upright) or rest between reps.
cues: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — this better targets the rear deltoids
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top of each rep
Keep the movement smooth and controlled — do not swing or use momentum
modification: Perform seated, leaning forward with chest resting on thighs — this removes the hip-hinge hold and allows you to focus purely on the arm movement.

name: Pallof Press
instructions: Anchor a resistance band at chest height to a door frame or sturdy post. Stand sideways to the anchor point about 2–3 feet away. Hold the band with both hands at your chest, fingers interlaced.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, core braced. The band will be pulling you toward the anchor — your job is to resist that pull throughout the exercise.

Take a breath in. As you exhale, press both hands straight out in front of you until your arms are fully extended. The band will try to rotate your torso toward the anchor — resist this completely by keeping your hips and shoulders square.
Hold the extended position for 2 seconds, then inhale and bring your hands back to your chest. This is one rep.
Complete all reps on one side, then turn around to work the other side. The challenge should feel equal on both sides.
cues: Your torso should not rotate at all — this anti-rotation is the entire point of the exercise
Brace your core like you are bracing for a punch before you press out
Keep your hips level and feet grounded — do not let one heel lift
modification: Use a lighter resistance band and stand closer to the anchor point to reduce the rotational pull.

name: Banded Glute Bridge
instructions: Place a resistance loop band just above your knees. Lie on your back with knees bent, feet flat on the floor hip-width apart, and arms at your sides.
Press your knees gently outward against the band so they track directly over your ankles (not caving inward). Maintain this outward pressure throughout the entire exercise.
Press your lower back gently into the mat, then drive through both heels and squeeze your glutes to lift your hips off the floor.
Rise until your hips form a straight line with your knees and shoulders. Squeeze your glutes hard at the top and keep pressing your knees out against the band.
Hold for 1–2 seconds at the top, then slowly lower your hips back to the mat with control. The band adds continuous hip abductor activation throughout the movement.
cues: Push your knees outward against the band throughout — this activates your glute medius
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top, not just lift your hips
Drive through your heels — wiggling your toes should not affect your ability to lift
modification: Use a lighter resistance band or remove the band and perform a standard glute bridge if the abductor demand is too high.

name: Clamshell
instructions: Place a resistance loop band just above your knees. Lie on your side on the mat with your hips stacked, knees bent to about 45 degrees, feet together. Support your head with your lower arm.
Keep your feet together throughout the movement. Take a breath in. As you exhale, lift your top knee upward toward the ceiling, rotating from the hip — like a clamshell opening.
Open as far as you can without your hips rolling backward. Your pelvis should stay completely still — place your top hand on your hip to monitor for rocking.
Pause at the top for one second, feeling the outer glute working. Then inhale and slowly lower your knee back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side, then switch sides.
cues: Keep your feet together — the rotation comes from the hip, not the foot separating
Pelvis stays still — if your hips rock back, you have gone too far
You should feel this in the outer glute, not your hip flexors or lower back
modification: Remove the resistance band and perform with bodyweight only, focusing on perfect form before adding resistance.

name: Banded Lateral Walk
instructions: Place a resistance loop band just above your ankles. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart with slight tension already in the band. Soften your knees into a mini-squat position — about a quarter-squat depth.
Keep your chest up and core braced. Take a step to the right with your right foot — about shoulder-width plus a few inches. Maintain the quarter-squat position throughout.
Bring your left foot in to follow, but do not let it come all the way in — keep enough distance to maintain band tension. Never let your feet come together.
Continue stepping right for the full set (12 steps right = one set), then reverse direction and walk left for another 12 steps.
Stay low in the squat position for the entire set. The moment you stand tall, the exercise becomes much easier and less effective.
cues: Stay low throughout — do not stand up and re-squat with each step
Keep toes pointing forward — do not let your feet angle outward as you tire
Control each step — do not snap the band by stepping too fast
modification: Use a lighter band or place the band above the knees instead of above the ankles to reduce resistance.

name: Banded Pull-Apart
instructions: Stand tall with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a resistance band in front of you at chest height with both hands, palms facing down. Your hands should be shoulder-width apart with slight tension already in the band.
Keep your arms straight. Take a breath in. As you exhale, pull the band apart by moving your hands outward and backward, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pull until the band touches your chest (or as far as your range of motion allows) and your arms are out to your sides. Squeeze your shoulder blades together firmly at this end position.
Hold for one second, feeling the contraction in your upper back and rear delts. Then inhale and slowly return to the starting position with control.
Keep your arms as straight as possible throughout — avoid bending the elbows to compensate.
cues: Keep arms straight and initiate the pull from your shoulder blades, not your biceps
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end range — that is where the benefit is
Stand tall — do not lean back or shrug your shoulders to pull the band
modification: Use a lighter band or widen your grip (hands further apart) to reduce resistance.

name: Banded Face Pull
instructions: Anchor a resistance band at face height (around a door handle, post, or loop it over a door). Stand facing the anchor, holding the band with both hands, palms facing down. Step back until there is good tension in the band.
Start with arms extended in front of you. Take a breath in. As you exhale, pull the band directly toward your face, separating your hands as you pull (pull to ear level, not chin level).
As you pull, externally rotate your shoulders so your elbows flare out to the sides and slightly upward, and your hands come to either side of your face with palms facing forward at the end.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold for one second at the end position. Your elbows should be higher than your wrists.
Inhale and slowly return to the starting position with full control.
cues: Pull toward your ears (or nose level), not your chin — too low loses the rear delt benefit
Elbows flare out and up — your upper arms should be roughly parallel to the floor at the end
Keep your torso still — no leaning back to help pull the band
modification: Use a lighter band or stand closer to the anchor point. Perform seated if balance is a challenge.

name: Side Plank
instructions: Lie on your right side with your legs straight and stacked on top of each other. Place your right forearm on the mat, elbow directly under your right shoulder.

Engage your core and lift your hips off the floor, creating a straight diagonal line from your head through your hips to your feet. Your body should form a plank — do not let your hips sag toward the floor.
Stack your feet or place the top foot slightly in front of the bottom foot for balance. Your top arm can rest on your hip or extend toward the ceiling.
Hold this position for 20 seconds. Breathe steadily — do not hold your breath. Keep your hips up and your core braced throughout.
Lower with control and repeat on the left side. Each side counts as one set.
cues: Keep hips lifted — hip sag removes the anti-lateral flexion challenge
Press your forearm into the mat and think of pushing the floor away to engage your shoulder
Breathe normally — holding your breath increases internal pressure unnecessarily
modification: Perform with knees bent and stacked — the bottom knee acts as a kickstand reducing the load significantly.

name: Forearm Plank
instructions: Start by kneeling on the mat. Place your forearms on the mat shoulder-width apart, elbows directly under your shoulders. Clasp your hands together or keep forearms parallel.
Step your feet back, one at a time, until your body forms a straight line from head to heels. Your toes are curled under on the mat.
Brace your core by drawing your belly button in slightly and squeezing your glutes. Your hips should be level — not raised into the air or sagging toward the floor.
Hold this position for 25 seconds, breathing steadily through your nose. Keep your head neutral — do not look up or drop your chin into your chest.
Lower to your knees with control when the time is up. Rest and repeat.
cues: Squeeze your glutes — this protects your lower back and stabilizes your pelvis
Straight line from head to heels — use a mirror or ask someone to check your form
Push your forearms into the floor — this activates your shoulder stabilizers and prevents sagging
modification: Drop to your knees for a forearm plank from knees. Keep your hips in line with your knees and shoulders.

name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Extend your right leg straight up toward the ceiling (or keep a slight bend in the knee).
Press your lower back into the mat and brace your core. Drive through your left heel and squeeze your left glute to lift your hips off the floor.
Rise until your body forms a straight line from your left knee through your hips to your shoulders. Your hips should stay level — resist the temptation to let the right side drop lower.
Hold at the top for 2 seconds, squeezing your glute. Then lower your hips slowly back to the mat with control.
Complete all reps on the left side, then switch: right foot drives, left leg extends. Count each side separately.
cues: Keep your hips level — the raised-leg side will want to dip, so consciously push both hips to the same height
Drive through the planted heel, not the ball of the foot
This is much harder than a standard bridge — reduce reps if needed to maintain perfect form
modification: Keep the extended leg bent at 90 degrees (foot toward ceiling) rather than straight to reduce the balance challenge.

name: Glute Bridge Isometric Hold
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Arms rest at your sides.
Press your lower back gently into the mat. Drive through both heels and squeeze your glutes to lift your hips off the floor until you form a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Hold this top position for the full 30 seconds. Keep squeezing your glutes continuously — do not relax them mid-hold. Breathe steadily.
Keep your hips level throughout — do not let one side drop. Your core should be actively engaged.
Lower slowly after the hold. Rest and repeat.
cues: Keep squeezing your glutes for the entire hold — this is an active contraction, not a passive hang
Breathe — inhale through your nose, exhale through your mouth, do not hold your breath
If your hips start to shake or drop, you have reached your limit — lower with control
modification: Reduce the hold to 15 seconds and build up over time.

name: Modified Pigeon Pose
instructions: Lie on your back with both knees bent and feet flat on the floor. This is the supine (lying-down) version of pigeon, which is safer for the lower back than the traditional floor version.
Cross your right ankle over your left thigh, just above the knee. Flex your right foot (point toes toward your shin) to protect your knee joint.
Reach both hands behind your left thigh and gently draw your left leg toward your chest. You will feel a deep stretch in the outer right hip and glute.
Hold this position for 40 seconds, breathing slowly and deeply. With each exhale, allow your body to relax into the stretch a little more — do not force it.
Lower both feet back to the floor slowly and repeat on the other side (left ankle over right thigh).
cues: Keep your right foot flexed throughout to protect the knee joint — do not let the foot go limp
Breathe into the stretch — tension in your breath creates tension in the muscle
If you cannot reach your thigh comfortably, loop a towel or strap around it
modification: Keep the bottom foot on the floor (without pulling the leg in) to reduce the intensity of the stretch.

name: Supine Figure-4 Stretch
instructions: Lie on your back with both knees bent, feet flat on the floor. Cross your right ankle over your left knee to form a figure-4 shape. Flex your right foot.
Let your right knee fall gently to the right — you should feel an immediate stretch in the outer right hip. If this is already deep enough, stay here.

For a deeper stretch: Reach your right hand through the gap between your legs and your left hand around the outside of your left thigh. Clasp your hands behind your left thigh and gently pull it toward your chest.
Hold for 40 seconds, breathing deeply. Your entire back stays flat on the mat throughout — do not let your hips rock to one side.
Release and switch sides: left ankle over right knee.
cues: Flex your top foot (toes toward shin) to protect the knee joint
Your back stays flat on the mat — do not let your hips twist
This targets the piriformis — a small muscle deep in the glute that can contribute to sciatic irritation
modification: Keep the bottom foot on the floor without pulling in and just let the crossed-knee drop open passively.

name: Child's Pose
instructions: Kneel on your mat with your big toes touching and knees apart (wider for a hip-opening variation, together for a lower back focus). Sit back onto your heels.
Walk your hands forward as far as you can, lowering your chest toward the floor between your thighs. Let your forehead rest on the mat.
Extend your arms as far forward as comfortable, feeling a lengthening through your spine and a gentle opening through your hips and lower back.
Hold for 40 seconds, breathing deeply. Each exhale, allow your hips to sink a little heavier toward your heels and your chest a little lower toward the floor.
To come out, walk your hands back and slowly rise up, supporting yourself with your hands.
cues: Allow gravity to do the work — this is a passive, restful stretch, not an active effort
Breathe into your back body — try to feel your back ribs expand with each inhale
Wider knees gives more hip opening; narrower knees focuses the stretch more on the spine
modification: Place a folded blanket or pillow between your thighs and calves if sitting back onto heels is uncomfortable.

name: Doorway Chest Stretch
instructions: Stand in a doorway. Raise both arms to shoulder height and bend your elbows to 90 degrees, placing your forearms vertically against the door frame on each side.
Step one foot forward through the doorway so you are in a staggered stance. Keep your core lightly engaged and spine tall.
Gently lean your body forward through the doorway until you feel a stretch across your chest and the front of your shoulders. Do not push aggressively — let the stretch come gradually.
Hold for 30 seconds. Breathe steadily and relax into the stretch with each exhale. Your forearms stay on the door frame throughout.
Step back to release. You can also perform this one arm at a time for a unilateral variation.
cues: Keep your elbows at shoulder height — lower elbows target the lower pec, higher elbows target the upper pec
Lean forward gently — this is not a stretch you force; let gravity and your lean do the work
Stand tall — do not let your lower back arch excessively as you lean
modification: Perform with one arm at a time using a wall corner if a full doorway is not available.

name: Seated Hamstring Stretch
instructions: Sit on the floor on your mat with both legs extended straight out in front of you. Sit up as tall as possible — if your back rounds heavily, sit on a folded blanket or towel to tilt your pelvis forward.
Flex both feet so your toes point toward the ceiling. Take a breath in and sit even taller through your spine.
As you exhale, hinge forward from your hips (not by rounding your back) to reach your hands toward your feet. Reach as far as comfortable — your shins, ankles, or feet.
Hold this position for 40 seconds, keeping a long spine. Breathe steadily — with each exhale, try to hinge a little further forward from the hip.
To come out, press your hands into the floor beside you and sit up slowly.
cues: Hinge from your hips, not your back — think of your torso tilting forward as a unit, spine long
If you can barely reach your shins, that is perfectly fine — hamstring flexibility takes weeks to build
Flex your feet actively — pointing toes reduces the hamstring stretch
modification: Loop a towel or resistance band around your feet and hold the ends to bridge the gap if you cannot reach your feet.

name: Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch
instructions: Kneel on your mat with your right knee on the floor directly under your right hip. Step your left foot forward so your left knee is at 90 degrees directly over your left ankle — a half-kneeling position. Use a folded blanket under your right knee if it is uncomfortable.

Sit tall, lengthen your spine, and gently tuck your pelvis slightly under (posterior pelvic tilt) — this is the key step that actually creates the hip flexor stretch. Without the tuck, the stretch is minimal.
Shift your hips slightly forward (not by leaning your torso, but by sliding your hips) until you feel a stretch in the front of your right hip.
Hold for 40 seconds. Breathe steadily and try to deepen the pelvic tuck slightly with each exhale to intensify the stretch.
Switch sides: left knee on floor, right foot forward.
cues: Tuck your pelvis under (flatten your lower back) — this is what creates the stretch, not just leaning forward
Keep your torso upright — do not lean your chest toward your front knee
The stretch should be felt in the front of the back-leg hip, not in the front knee
modification: Place a folded towel under your kneeling knee for cushioning. Reduce the forward shift if the stretch is too intense.

name: Standing Quad Stretch
instructions: Stand tall near a wall or chair for balance support. Shift your weight onto your left foot.

Bend your right knee and bring your right heel toward your right glute. Reach back with your right hand and hold your right ankle or the top of your foot. If you cannot reach, loop a towel around your ankle.
Keep your knees together — your right knee should point straight down toward the floor, not out to the side. Keep your torso upright and core lightly engaged.
Hold for 30 seconds. You should feel a stretch along the front of your right thigh. Avoid pulling so hard that your knee lifts or your foot pulls away from center.
Lower your foot and switch sides.
cues: Keep both knees side by side — pulling your knee out to the side changes the stretch target
Stand tall — do not lean forward or arch your lower back
Use a wall for balance; wobbling reduces the quality of the stretch
modification: Lie face-down on the mat and bend one knee, reaching back to hold the ankle — this removes the balance challenge entirely.

name: Banded Lat Pulldown
instructions: Anchor a resistance band overhead — loop it over a door frame, pull-up bar, or secure it at the top of a door. Kneel or sit on the floor far enough away that there is good tension in the band with arms extended overhead.
Grip the band with both hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, palms facing forward. Kneel or sit tall with your spine neutral and core lightly engaged.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, drive both elbows down and back toward your hips — imagine trying to put your elbows in your back pockets. Pull the band toward your upper chest or collarbone.
At the bottom of the movement, squeeze your lats (the muscles just under your armpits) firmly and hold for one second. Your shoulder blades should be depressed (pulled down) and pulled together.
Inhale and slowly return your arms overhead with control — do not let the band yank your arms up. Reach full extension at the top before beginning the next rep.
cues: Drive elbows down toward your hips — not backward behind your body
Think of pulling your shoulder blades into your back pockets at the bottom
Lean back very slightly (10–15 degrees) — this puts your lats in a better pulling angle
modification: Widen your grip on the band or anchor it lower to reduce resistance. Alternatively, perform one arm at a time.

name: Prone Y-T-W
instructions: Lie face down on your mat with your forehead resting on a folded towel or your hands. Engage your core lightly to protect your lower back throughout all three positions.
Y position: Raise both arms overhead in a wide V shape with thumbs pointing toward the ceiling. Lift just your arms off the mat 2–3 inches, hold 2 seconds, lower. This targets the lower trapezius.

T position: Bring your arms straight out to your sides at shoulder height, forming a T shape with thumbs up. Lift both arms 2–3 inches, hold 2 seconds, lower. This targets the middle trapezius and rear deltoids.

W position: Bend your elbows to 90 degrees and raise them beside your head in a W shape with elbows wide. Squeeze your shoulder blades together as you lift. Hold 2 seconds, lower. This targets the rotator cuff and lower trap.

Complete 8 reps of each shape (Y then T then W) in sequence — that is one full set. Move slowly and with control. The range of motion is small; only lift as high as you can without straining your neck.
cues: Thumbs point toward the ceiling in each position — this externally rotates the shoulder
Only lift your arms — do not arch your lower back or lift your chest off the mat
Movements are small and controlled — this is shoulder activation work, not a power exercise
modification: Perform one arm at a time, resting the other at your side. Reduce the hold time to 1 second if your shoulder fatigues quickly.

name: Supine Spinal Twist
instructions: Lie on your back on the mat with both legs straight. Draw your right knee in toward your chest and hold it briefly.
Cross your right knee over your body to the left side, guiding it gently with your left hand. Aim to bring your right knee toward the floor on the left side — it does not need to touch the floor.
Extend your right arm straight out to the right at shoulder height, palm facing up. Turn your head to look toward your right hand. This is the full twist position.

Hold for 40 seconds, breathing slowly and deeply. With each exhale, allow the twist to deepen slightly. Your right shoulder should remain in contact with the mat — if it lifts, reduce how far the knee crosses.
Release slowly and switch sides: left knee crosses to the right, left arm extends, head turns left.
cues: Keep both shoulders flat on the mat — if one lifts, reduce the depth of the twist
Breathe into the twist — tension in your breath creates tension in the muscle
Let gravity do the work — do not force the knee down with your hand
modification: Place a pillow or folded blanket under your crossed knee to reduce the depth of the twist if it feels too intense in the lower back.

name: 90/90 Hip Stretch
instructions: Sit on the floor on your mat. Bend your right leg in front of you with your shin roughly at a 90-degree angle to your thigh. Bend your left leg to the side with your shin also at 90 degrees behind you. Both hips should be on or close to the floor.
Sit as tall as possible. You will feel a stretch in the outside of your front hip (right glute and external rotators) and across the inside of your back hip (left hip flexor and internal rotators).

Place your hands on the floor beside you for support. Take a breath in and sit even taller through your spine. As you exhale, hinge slightly forward at the hip over your front leg — lean from your hip, not by rounding your back.
Hold for 40 seconds, breathing steadily. Focus on sitting tall and gradually relaxing into the stretch rather than forcing a deep fold.
To switch sides, lift both knees, pivot, and place your left shin in front and your right shin behind. Repeat on the other side.
cues: Keep both sit bones in contact with the floor — if one hip floats up, reduce the angle of the back leg
Sit tall before folding — rounding your lower back early takes the stretch away from the hips
This stretch targets both internal and external hip rotation simultaneously — normal to feel it in different spots
modification: Sit with your back against a wall for posture support. Place folded blankets under both thighs if your hips are very tight.

name: Single-Leg Balance Hold
instructions: Stand tall near a wall or stable surface within arm's reach for safety. Shift your weight onto your right foot and stand with your right knee very slightly soft — never fully locked.

Lift your left foot off the floor, bending the left knee to about 90 degrees so your left thigh is roughly parallel to the floor. Keep your hips level — resist the urge to let your left hip drop lower than your right.
Fix your gaze on a single stationary point at eye level. This focal point dramatically improves balance. Keep your arms relaxed at your sides.
Hold for 30 seconds without touching the wall. If you feel yourself tipping, allow a brief fingertip touch — the goal is to need it less and less over time. Breathe normally throughout.
Switch feet and repeat on the left leg. Both sides count as one set.
cues: Pick a focal point and do not look down — eyes down is the most common cause of wobbling
Keep your hips level — the raised-leg hip wants to drop, actively hold it even
Small ankle adjustments are normal and healthy — your ankle is supposed to be working
modification: Keep your fingertips lightly on the wall throughout. Progress to fingertip touch only, then no contact. For an advanced challenge, close your eyes.

name: Bodyweight Calf Raise
instructions: Stand with your feet hip-width apart near a wall or stable surface for light balance support. Place your fingertips lightly on the surface — do not hold or press, just use it for balance.
Press through the balls of both feet to rise onto your toes as high as possible. Pause at the top for one full second, feeling the contraction in your calves.
Slowly lower your heels back to the floor over 2–3 seconds. This slow lowering is the most important part — eccentric loading of the Achilles tendon is the primary strengthening stimulus.
Your heels should fully return to the floor between reps — do not bounce or spring from the bottom. A brief pause at the floor before each rep ensures full range of motion.
Complete 15 reps per set. As this becomes easy, progress to single-leg calf raises: same movement on one foot at a time with the other foot lightly resting against your ankle.
cues: Rise as high as you can — maximum height engages the full calf and Achilles range
Lower slowly — 2–3 seconds on the way down is where the real strengthening happens
Keep your knees straight throughout — bending them shifts the load from gastrocnemius to soleus
modification: Reduce to 10 reps per set. For greater range of motion, stand on the edge of a step with heels hanging slightly off.

name: TRX Row
instructions: Set TRX straps to mid-length. Stand facing the anchor point and hold both handles with an overhand grip, palms facing down. Walk your feet toward the anchor until your body is at about 45 degrees to the floor — the more horizontal, the harder the row.
Hang with arms fully extended, body in a straight line from heels to shoulders. Your heels are on the floor. Brace your core and squeeze your glutes to keep your body rigid like a plank.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, drive both elbows back toward your hips, rowing your chest up toward the handles. Keep your body in a straight plank throughout — do not let your hips sag or pike.
Pull until your chest reaches the handles and your elbows are behind your torso. Squeeze your shoulder blades together firmly at this top position and hold for one second.
Inhale and lower yourself back to the fully extended starting position with control. Your body stays rigid throughout — only your arms and shoulder blades should move.
cues: Keep your body in a rigid plank — if your hips drop, you lose the core challenge and reduce back activation
Drive your elbows toward your back pockets, not straight up toward the ceiling
Walk your feet in (more horizontal) to make it harder; walk feet back (more upright) to make it easier
modification: Walk your feet back toward vertical to reduce difficulty. Beginners should start at a 60-70 degree angle from the floor.

name: TRX Push-Up
instructions: Set TRX straps to mid-length with handles at about knee height. Face away from the anchor. Place both hands in the TRX handles, arms extended, and step back until you are in a high plank position at roughly 45 degrees to the floor.

Hands directly below shoulders, wrists neutral, core braced, body in a rigid line from head to heels. The handles will swing slightly — actively resist this instability with your core and shoulder stabilizers.
Take a breath in. Bend your elbows at roughly 45 degrees and lower your chest toward the handles in a controlled push-up. The straps will oscillate — resist this throughout.
Lower until your chest is level with the handles or your elbows reach 90 degrees. Do not let your hips sag or elbows flare wide.
Press forcefully back to the starting position, exhaling as you push. Fully extend your arms without locking your elbows. Reset your form before the next rep.
cues: Keep your body rigid throughout — TRX push-ups immediately expose any form breakdown
Elbows at 45 degrees, not flared wide — same mechanics as a floor push-up
Walk your feet further from the anchor (more upright) to make it easier if the instability is too great
modification: Walk your feet back away from the anchor to a more upright position, reducing the load significantly. Master floor push-ups first and use TRX as a progression.

name: Resistance Band Chest Press
instructions: Anchor your resistance band at chest height — loop it around a sturdy door frame post or use a door anchor. Hold one end in each hand. Stand facing away from the anchor, hands at chest height, elbows at 90 degrees at shoulder level.
Step forward until there is good tension in the band. Take a staggered stance for stability. Brace your core firmly — the band will try to pull your torso backward.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, press both hands forward, extending your arms straight ahead. Your hands should travel on a slight inward arc, coming closer together at the end of the press.
Fully extend your arms without locking your elbows. Pause briefly at the end, feeling the contraction across your chest.
Inhale and slowly return your hands to the starting position at your chest, controlling the band on the return. This is one rep.
cues: Keep your core braced throughout — the band wants to pull your torso backward; resist it
Press forward and slightly inward — bringing hands toward the center activates the chest more fully
Step further from the anchor for more resistance; step closer to reduce it
modification: Use a lighter band or stand closer to the anchor. Single-arm press is a good option to reduce total load while maintaining stability.

name: Resistance Band Overhead Press
instructions: Stand on the middle of your resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the band ends at shoulder height, palms facing forward, elbows at 90 degrees just below ear level.
Take a breath in and brace your core. Tuck your pelvis slightly to prevent your lower back from arching when you press overhead — this is the most important cue.
As you exhale, press both hands straight up overhead until your arms are nearly fully extended. The band increases resistance as you press — control the movement throughout.
Your arms should finish directly overhead, not angled forward. Pause briefly without locking your elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the band back to shoulder height over 2-3 seconds. This controlled descent works the shoulders equally to the press.
cues: Core braced and pelvis slightly tucked — do not arch your lower back to complete the press
Press directly overhead, not forward — your arms should finish directly above your ears
Stand on more of the band (feet wider apart) for more resistance; less for a lighter load
modification: Spread your feet wider on the band to reduce working length and resistance. Perform seated to remove lower back stability demands.

name: Resistance Band Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand on the middle of your resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold both ends at your sides, palms facing inward. Keep a very slight bend in your elbows throughout.
Take a breath in. Your starting position has your hands at your sides with light tension already in the band.

As you exhale, raise both arms out to your sides simultaneously, leading with your elbows. Think of pouring water from a jug — your pinky side leads slightly, which targets the lateral deltoid most effectively.
Raise until your arms are at shoulder height — parallel to the floor. Do not raise above this level.
Pause for one second at the top, then inhale and slowly lower your arms back to your sides over 2 seconds.
cues: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — this targets the lateral deltoid correctly
Stop at shoulder height — higher adds impingement risk without extra benefit
No shrugging or swinging; if you cannot control the movement, shorten the band length
modification: Stand on less of the band (near one end) to reduce resistance. Perform one arm at a time if needed for control.

name: Resistance Band Bicep Curl
instructions: Stand on the middle of your resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the band ends at your sides with palms facing forward, upper arms close to your sides.
Keep your upper arms completely still throughout — the movement comes only from your forearms rotating upward. Shoulders back and down before starting.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, curl both hands upward toward your shoulders by bending at the elbows. The band resistance increases as you curl — maintain control through the full range.
Curl until you feel a strong bicep contraction with forearms roughly vertical. Pause and squeeze for one second at the top.
Inhale and slowly lower the band back to the starting position over 2-3 seconds. The controlled lowering builds as much strength as the curl itself.
cues: Upper arms stay pinned to your sides throughout — only your forearms should move
Squeeze the bicep hard at the top before lowering
Slow on the way down — 2-3 seconds on the descent builds serious strength
modification: Stand on less of the band for lighter resistance. Perform one arm at a time to focus on each side individually.

name: Resistance Band Squat
instructions: Stand on the middle of your resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart, toes turned out 15-30 degrees. Hold the band ends at shoulder height with elbows pointing forward — a front-rack position. There should be light tension in the band when standing.
Take a breath in and brace your core. Begin the squat by pushing your hips back first, then bend your knees to lower yourself. Keep your elbows up and chest tall throughout.
Descend until your thighs reach parallel to the floor or as deep as comfortable. Your knees should track over your second toe. The band adds peak resistance as you stand back up.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then press through your full foot to drive back up to standing, exhaling on the way up.
Fully extend your hips and knees at the top before the next rep. The band provides increasing resistance through the standing phase, training the full range of the movement.
cues: Hips back first, then knees bend — keep your weight over your heels throughout
Elbows stay up — if they drop, your torso will fall forward
Push your knees actively outward as you squat — do not let them cave inward
modification: Squat to a chair or bench behind you for a depth reference. Hold the band at chest level with crossed arms if the front-rack position is uncomfortable.

name: Resistance Band Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand on the middle of your resistance band with feet hip-width apart. Hold the band ends in each hand at your sides, palms facing inward. Stand tall with a neutral spine and core braced.

Take a breath in. Begin the hinge by pushing your hips straight back — think of reaching your glutes toward the wall behind you. Your spine stays completely long and neutral as your torso tips forward.

As your hips push back, your torso tips forward and the band travels down the front of your thighs. Keep the band close to your body throughout. The band stretches and increases resistance as you hinge.
Lower until you feel a strong hamstring stretch — typically when your torso is roughly parallel to the floor. Do not go past where you can maintain a neutral spine. Pause briefly.
Exhale and drive your hips forward, squeezing your glutes aggressively as you return to standing. Think 'hips forward' not 'lift up' — the power comes from the glutes completing the hip extension.
cues: Push your hips backward, not your body downward — this is a hinge, not a squat
Keep the band dragging along your legs — letting it drift away shifts load to your lower back
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top — full hip extension is what makes this an RDL
modification: Reduce the range of motion to where you can comfortably maintain a neutral spine. Place a hand lightly on a wall for balance support if needed.

name: Resistance Band Tricep Pushdown
instructions: Anchor your resistance band at shoulder height or above — loop it over a door frame, hook, or door anchor. Hold both ends of the band with an overhand grip, elbows bent at 90 degrees and pinned to your sides. Stand close enough to maintain tension.
Pin your upper arms to your sides for the entire exercise — they are the pivot point and must not move. Only your forearms extend downward.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, push both hands downward by straightening your elbows against the band resistance. Extend until your arms are fully straight alongside your thighs.
Squeeze your triceps hard at full extension for one second. Full extension is where the tricep contraction is greatest.
Inhale and slowly allow the band to bring your forearms back up to the 90-degree starting position. Control the return — do not let it snap up. This is one rep.
cues: Upper arms stay pinned to your sides — only your forearms move from the elbow down
Fully extend for the complete tricep contraction — partial reps reduce the benefit
Stand closer to the anchor to reduce resistance; step back to increase it
modification: Use a lighter band or perform one arm at a time. Anchor the band lower if needed.

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie flat on a gym bench with feet flat on the floor. Hold a dumbbell in each hand at chest level, elbows at roughly 45-50 degrees from your torso. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and press them into the bench before you start.
Take a breath in and brace your core. Press your feet firmly into the floor to create full-body tension — this protects your shoulders and improves pressing power.
As you exhale, press both dumbbells upward and slightly inward in a gentle arc, extending your arms until nearly locked out above your chest. The dumbbells should nearly touch at the top.
Pause briefly at the top. Maintain your shoulder blade squeeze throughout — do not let your shoulders roll forward.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells with control back to chest level over 2-3 seconds, elbows at 45-50 degrees. Touch the sides of your chest lightly before pressing again.
cues: Shoulder blades squeezed together and pressed into the bench — this protects your shoulders and increases pressing power
Elbows at 45-50 degrees — not flared wide (AC joint stress) or tucked too tight
Press up and slightly inward — the inward arc at the top increases chest activation
modification: Use lighter dumbbells (10-15 lb) to start. Perform a floor chest press if you are not comfortable loading heavy dumbbells on a bench alone.

name: Chest Press Machine
instructions: Sit in the chest press machine. Adjust the seat height so the handles are at chest level — roughly nipple height. Adjust the starting position so your elbows are slightly behind your torso when gripping, creating a stretch across your chest.
Feet flat on the floor, back pressed firmly against the seat pad. Grip the handles at a comfortable width. Squeeze your shoulder blades back against the pad.

Take a breath in. As you exhale, press both handles forward by extending your elbows. Push until your arms are nearly fully extended — do not lock your elbows. Keep your back and head against the pad throughout.
Pause briefly at the extended position, feeling the chest contraction.
Inhale and slowly return the handles toward you over 2-3 seconds. Lower until you feel a good chest stretch — your elbows should travel slightly behind your torso. This is one rep.
cues: Keep your back pressed against the pad throughout — avoid arching away from the seat
Control the return — 2-3 seconds back maximizes chest engagement
Squeeze your shoulder blades together before pressing — this creates a stable base and protects the shoulder joint
modification: Reduce the machine weight and use a limited range of motion if shoulder discomfort occurs. Ask gym staff to help set the seat position on your first use.

name: Machine Shoulder Press
instructions: Sit in the shoulder press machine and adjust the seat so the handles are at about ear level with your elbows at 90 degrees. Feet flat on the floor. Sit tall and press your back against the pad.
Grip the handles with palms facing forward. Take a breath in and brace your core lightly.
As you exhale, press both handles straight up overhead until your arms are nearly fully extended. The handles should travel directly above your shoulders, not forward in front of your head.
Pause briefly at the top. Look straight ahead — do not crane your neck upward.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to ear level over 2-3 seconds. Do not let the weight stack touch between reps — maintain constant tension throughout.
cues: Sit tall throughout — do not let your lower back arch away from the pad
Press directly overhead, not forward of your ears
2-second lowering phase doubles the effectiveness — resist the urge to let it drop
modification: Reduce the weight. Raise the seat if shoulder discomfort occurs at the starting position — higher seat reduces the external rotation demand.

name: Lat Pulldown Machine
instructions: Sit at the lat pulldown machine and adjust the knee pad so it rests firmly on your thighs, preventing you from rising when pulling. Grip the bar with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Sit down and secure your knees. Allow your arms to fully extend and your lats to stretch at the top.
Lean back very slightly — about 10-15 degrees — and brace your core. This slight lean puts your lats in a more efficient pulling angle. Fix your gaze at mid-chest level.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, drive both elbows down and toward your hips — think of putting your elbows in your back pockets. Pull the bar toward your upper chest.
Pull until the bar reaches your upper chest or collarbone. Squeeze your lats hard — the muscles just under your armpits — and hold for one second.
Inhale and slowly let the bar rise back to the start over 2-3 seconds. Allow your arms to fully extend and your lats to stretch at the top before the next rep.
cues: Drive elbows down toward your hips, not straight back — this maximizes lat engagement over bicep use
Do not lean back more than 15 degrees — excessive lean turns it into a row
Full extension at the top matters — the lat stretch at the top is where development happens
modification: Reduce the weight. A neutral-grip (palms facing each other) bar attachment is more comfortable for beginners and equally effective.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
instructions: Place your left knee and left hand on a flat gym bench for support. Torso parallel to the floor, neutral spine. Hold a dumbbell in your right hand at arm's length directly below your right shoulder.
Squeeze your right shoulder blade inward and down to create initial tension. Take a breath in and brace your core.
As you exhale, row the dumbbell upward by driving your right elbow toward the ceiling and back. Keep the dumbbell close to your body throughout.
Pull until your right elbow is clearly above the level of your back and you feel a strong contraction in your lat and upper back. Squeeze your shoulder blade toward your spine at the top.
Inhale and lower the dumbbell back to the starting position with full control over 2 seconds. Complete all reps on the right side, then switch to the left.
cues: Drive your elbow up and back — the dumbbell follows the elbow, not the other way around
Squeeze your shoulder blade at the top — if you don't feel your back, the bicep is doing all the work
Keep your torso parallel to the floor — rotating your shoulders to help the weight up is cheating
modification: Use a lighter dumbbell. Brace one hand on a countertop or rack with both feet on the floor if no bench is available.

name: Cable Face Pull
instructions: Set a cable machine pulley with a rope attachment to approximately face height. Stand facing the machine and hold the rope with both hands, thumbs pointing up. Step back until there is tension in the cable with arms extended in front of you.
Take a light staggered stance for stability. Starting position: arms extended, rope at face height, core lightly braced.

Take a breath in. As you exhale, pull the rope toward your face by driving both elbows out to the sides and slightly upward. Split your hands apart as you pull — one end toward each side of your face.

Externally rotate your shoulders as you pull: elbows flare out and hands end up beside your temples with palms facing forward. Your upper arms should be roughly parallel to the floor at the end. Squeeze your rear delts and rhomboids.
Hold the contracted position for one second. Inhale and slowly return to the starting extended position. This is one rep.
cues: Pull toward your nose or ears, not your chin — lower trajectory loses the rear delt and rotator cuff benefit
Elbows flare out and UP at the end, not straight back
Keep your torso still throughout — no leaning backward to help the weight
modification: Reduce the cable weight. Stand closer to the machine to reduce effective resistance. A rope attachment is strongly preferred over a straight bar.

name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs, palms facing inward. Feet hip-width apart, knees soft. Shoulders retracted, chest tall, core braced.
Take a breath in. Begin the hinge by pushing your hips straight back — think of reaching your glutes toward the wall behind you. Your spine stays completely long and neutral throughout.

As your hips push back, your torso tips forward and the dumbbells travel down the front of your thighs and shins. Keep the dumbbells in contact with or very close to your legs to maintain proper center of gravity.
Lower until you feel a strong hamstring stretch — typically mid-shin depending on flexibility. Do not go past where you can maintain a neutral spine. Pause briefly.
Exhale and drive your hips forward, squeezing your glutes aggressively as you stand back up. Think 'hips forward' not 'lift the weight' — the glute squeeze completes the hip extension.
cues: Hips go backward first — this is a hip hinge, not a squat or a forward bend
Keep the dumbbells dragging along your legs — drifting forward shifts dangerous load to the lumbar spine
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top — this is what completes the movement and protects the lower back
modification: Use lighter dumbbells and reduce range of motion to where you can perfectly maintain spinal alignment. Perform in front of a mirror to check your form.

name: Lying Leg Curl Machine
instructions: Lie face-down on the leg curl machine. Adjust the ankle pad so it rests just above your heels on the lower Achilles area. Your knees should be just off the edge of the bench when legs are straight. Grip the handles under the bench for stability.
Press your hips firmly into the pad throughout the movement. Starting position: legs fully extended.
As you exhale, curl both legs simultaneously by bending your knees, pulling your heels toward your glutes against the machine's resistance.
Curl until your knees are fully bent as far as the machine allows. Squeeze your hamstrings hard at this contracted position for one second.
Inhale and slowly extend your legs back to the starting position over 2-3 seconds. Do not let the weight stack bang down. Maintain controlled tension throughout the return.
cues: Keep your hips pressed into the pad — if they lift, the weight is too heavy
Full range: straight to fully bent — partial curls significantly reduce effectiveness
Slow on the way down — the controlled lowering phase builds hamstring resilience
modification: Reduce the machine weight significantly on your first session — leg curl machines vary widely between brands. A seated leg curl machine is an alternative if lying face-down causes discomfort.

name: Leg Press Machine
instructions: Sit in the leg press machine with your back and hips pressed firmly against the back pad. Feet hip-width apart on the footplate, toes turned out 15-20 degrees, positioned mid-plate. Unlock the safety handles before starting.
Starting position: knees at roughly 90 degrees. Your lower back should not peel away from the back pad at your working depth — if it does, reduce your range of motion.

Take a breath in and brace your core. As you exhale, press through your full foot to extend your legs and push the footplate away. Keep a soft bend in your knees at the top — do not fully lock them out.
Press until your legs are nearly extended (about 170 degrees). Pause briefly.
Inhale and slowly lower the footplate back to your starting depth over 2-3 seconds. Re-engage the safety handles before exiting the machine.
cues: Never lock your knees at the top — always maintain a soft, slight bend
Keep your back pressed against the pad throughout — if it peels away, you have gone too deep
Press through your full foot, heels included — this better targets the glutes
modification: Start with a very light weight to find your comfortable range. Wider foot placement targets glutes more; closer/lower placement targets quads.

name: Kettlebell Swing
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly outward. Place a kettlebell on the floor about a foot in front of you. Hinge at the hips to grip the handle with both hands, arms inside your legs. Back flat, core braced, hips higher than knees.
Hike the kettlebell back between your legs with a sharp hip hinge — like a center snap in football. This backswing loads your hamstrings and creates the stretch reflex you will convert into power.

As the kettlebell swings back and your hips are fully loaded, snap your hips forward aggressively and squeeze your glutes. This explosive hip drive is the power source — your arms are just a pendulum.
The kettlebell floats to chest or shoulder height. At the top, your body is fully upright, glutes squeezed hard, core braced — a standing plank. Do not lean back.
As the kettlebell descends, hinge your hips back again to receive it and guide it between your legs. This is a continuous, fluid motion. Complete all reps without stopping.
cues: The power comes from snapping your hips forward, not from swinging your arms — your arms are a rope, your hips are the engine
Hinge, do not squat — on the backswing your hips go back, not down
Stand completely tall and squeeze your glutes hard at the top of each swing
modification: Use a lighter kettlebell (8-12 kg for beginners). Practice the hip hinge first: stand arm's length from a wall, push your glutes back to touch it, then drive forward.

name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat
instructions: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, toes turned out 30-45 degrees. Hold a kettlebell by both sides of the handle (the horns) at your chest, close to your sternum. Elbows point down.
Take a breath in and brace your core. Begin by pushing your hips back and out, then bend your knees to descend between your legs. The kettlebell stays close to your chest throughout.
Descend as deep as comfortable — ideally thighs parallel or below. As you descend, use your elbows to push your knees outward, opening your hips for depth. Heels stay flat on the floor.
Pause briefly at the bottom. Drive through your full foot to press back up to standing, exhaling on the way up. Squeeze your glutes at the top.
The front-loaded kettlebell naturally keeps your torso upright — this is what makes goblet squats excellent for teaching squat mechanics.
cues: Keep the kettlebell close to your chest — letting it drift forward pulls you forward
Use your elbows to push your knees outward as you descend — this opens the hips
Heels stay flat on the floor throughout — if they rise, widen your stance or work on ankle mobility
modification: Squat to a bench or box behind you as a depth reference. Use a lighter kettlebell (8-12 kg for beginners).

name: Dumbbell Walking Lunge
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Find a clear path of about 10 metres. Chest up, shoulders back throughout.
Step your right foot forward, taking a large step. Land with your right foot flat and your right knee tracking directly over your right ankle — not pushing past your toes.
Lower your back (left) knee toward the floor by bending both knees simultaneously. Stop when your back knee is about 1-2 inches from the floor. Both knees should reach roughly 90 degrees.
Drive through your right heel and step your left foot forward past your right foot into the next lunge step. This continuous walking motion continues for the full set.
