Continue alternating legs for 10 steps per side (20 total steps). Keep your torso completely upright throughout — do not lean over your front thigh. Dumbbells hang at your sides.
cues: Stay completely tall — the most common error is the torso leaning forward over the front thigh
Front knee over ankle — step far enough forward that your knee does not go past your toes
Control the descent — 2 seconds down prevents the back knee from crashing to the floor
modification: Perform static lunges (step forward and drive back to start) instead of walking lunges. Reduce dumbbell weight or use bodyweight only.

name: Seated Calf Raise Machine
instructions: Sit at the seated calf raise machine. Adjust the knee pad so it rests firmly just above your knees. Place the balls of your feet on the footplate edge with heels hanging off. Starting position: heels lowered as far as comfortable into a full stretch.
From the fully stretched position, press through the balls of both feet to rise as high as possible, lifting your heels. Rise as high as the machine allows.
Hold at the top for 1-2 seconds, feeling the full calf contraction. Do not cut the range short.
Slowly lower your heels back down over 2-3 seconds, allowing the calves to fully stretch at the bottom. The eccentric (lowering) phase is critical for calf and Achilles strengthening.
Pause briefly in the fully stretched position before the next rep. Do not bounce from the bottom.
cues: Full range of motion both ways: maximum stretch at the bottom, maximum height at the top
Slow on the way down — 2-3 seconds on the lowering phase is where the Achilles strengthening occurs
Keep your knee angle constant — do not let your knees rise or dip as you move
modification: Perform bodyweight standing calf raises on the edge of a step if no machine is available.

name: Cable Tricep Pushdown
instructions: Set a cable machine with a straight bar or rope attachment at a high position. Face the machine and hold the attachment with both hands, shoulder-width apart, overhand grip. Step back slightly for cable tension.
Bring your elbows to your sides, bent at 90 degrees. This is your starting position. Your elbows stay pinned to your sides throughout — they are the pivot point.
Take a breath in. As you exhale, push the bar or rope downward by extending your elbows against the cable resistance.
Extend until your arms are fully straight alongside your thighs — elbows locked out. Squeeze your triceps hard at this fully extended position for one second.
Inhale and slowly allow the weight to bring your forearms back up to the 90-degree starting position over 2 seconds. Do not let your elbows drift forward during the return.
cues: Elbows stay pinned at your sides throughout — when elbows drift forward, load shifts off the triceps
Full extension: lock out at the bottom for maximum tricep contraction
Stand closer to the stack if the cable is pulling you onto your toes
modification: Reduce the cable weight. A rope attachment (where you can flare the ends out at the bottom) is preferred for achieving full extension.

name: Treadmill Walk / Run
instructions: Set the treadmill to a moderate pace that gets your heart rate up but still allows short sentences — typically 3.5-4.5 mph for a brisk walk or 5-6 mph for a light jog. For a greater challenge without more speed, add a 2-4% incline.
Step on, stand tall, and let the belt bring you into your stride. Chest lifted, arms swinging naturally. Avoid holding onto the handrails — it reduces calorie burn and disrupts natural gait.
Breathe steadily. At a good challenge pace, you should be able to say a short sentence but not hold a full conversation.
Maintain this pace for the full 10 minutes. This is the cardiovascular finish of your circuit — aim for a satisfying challenge rather than easy movement.
In the final 1-2 minutes, gradually reduce your speed as a natural cool-down. Step off carefully only when the belt has slowed.
cues: Look forward, not down at your feet — looking down promotes a hunched, inefficient posture
Do not hold the handrails — it significantly reduces the workout and disrupts natural movement
Add incline to increase intensity without increasing impact if joint discomfort is a concern
modification: Walk at a slower pace (2.5-3.5 mph) or reduce to 5 minutes if 10 feels too challenging. Flat setting is fine — incline is optional.

name: Rowing Machine Warm-Up
instructions: Sit on the rowing machine and strap your feet in securely. Grip the handle with both hands, arms fully extended and relaxed. Sit with a neutral spine, knees bent, shins roughly vertical. This is the catch position.

Begin with an easy, light row. The stroke has four phases in sequence: push with legs, lean back slightly, pull arms to your lower ribs — then reverse: arms extend, lean forward, knees bend. Follow this sequence every stroke.

For the warm-up, row at approximately 18-22 strokes per minute — an unhurried, comfortable pace. Focus on smooth technique rather than power: legs push first (60% of power), then hips hinge back slightly, then arms pull.
Breathe in sync: exhale on the drive (power phase), inhale on the recovery (return phase). Keep your shoulders relaxed and down throughout.
After 5 minutes your joints should feel warm and loose. Ease off in the final 30 seconds.
cues: Legs first, then lean, then arms — this stroke sequence is fundamental
Legs generate most of the power (about 60%) — do not skip straight to pulling with your arms
Keep your grip relaxed — squeezing too tight fatigues the forearms quickly
modification: Row at a slower pace (16-18 SPM) if 5 minutes feels challenging. Focus entirely on learning the stroke sequence rather than the pace.

name: Elliptical Warm-Up
instructions: Step onto the elliptical and place your feet in the pedals. Grip the moving handles lightly. Start pedaling forward at a low resistance level (1-3 on most machines).
Find a comfortable rhythm — aim for about 60-70 RPM. The elliptical simulates a running motion with zero joint impact, making it ideal for active recovery days.

Push through your heels and engage your glutes as you pedal — avoid riding entirely on the balls of your feet. Use the moving handles lightly to involve your upper body, or hold the stationary handles if preferred.
Breathe easily and steadily. On an active recovery day, keep your heart rate low-to-moderate (about 50-65% of your maximum). You should be able to hold a conversation comfortably.
After 10 minutes, gradually reduce resistance and slow your pace over the final minute as a natural transition into the mobility work that follows.
cues: Push through your heels to engage your glutes — pedaling from your toes bypasses the glutes entirely
Keep your chest lifted and core lightly engaged throughout
This is active recovery — keep the intensity genuinely low; today is about movement, not training
modification: Reduce to 5 minutes at an even lower resistance. A stationary bike is a fine alternative if the elliptical is unavailable.
